request.question: What is the most important building in the National Heritage for England located 4 mi southwest of Congleton in Cheshire?

title: Listed buildings in Appleby-in-Westmorland
Listed buildings in Odd Rode
Congleton United Reformed Church
Church of St Thomas à Becket, Capel
Listed buildings in Congleton
Listed buildings in Edge, Cheshire
All Saints Chapel, Somerford
Nether Alderley Mill
St Peter's Church, Congleton
Little Moreton Hall
sentences: Appleby-in-Westmorland is a civil parish in the Eden District, Cumbria, England.
It contains 140 buildings that are recorded in the National Heritage List for England.
Of these, six are listed at Grade I, the highest of the three grades, nine are at Grade II*, the middle grade, and the others are at Grade II, the lowest grade.
The parish contains the market town of Appleby (renamed Appleby-in-Westmorland in 1974) and surrounding countryside.
There is a great variety of types of listed building in the parish.
The most important building is Appleby Castle; this and a number of associated structures are listed.
Most of the listed buildings are houses and associated structures, shops and public buildings.

Other listed buildings include churches, public houses, hotels, two crosses and a lamp post, farmhouses and farm buildings, former industrial buildings, schools, banks, a bridge, railway station buildings, and a milestone.
Odd Rode is a civil parish in Cheshire East, England.

It contains 35 buildings that are recorded in the National Heritage List for England as designated listed buildings. Of these, one is listed at Grade I, the highest grade, five are listed at Grade II*, the middle grade, and the others are at Grade II.
The most important listed building in the parish is Little Moreton Hall; other notable country houses include Rode Hall and Ramsdell Hall.
Most of the other listed buildings are houses, farmhouses, and associated structures.
The Macclesfield and the Trent and Mersey Canals run through the parish, and the listed buildings associated with these are bridges, milestones, and distance markers.
The other listed buildings are a church.
a folly, wellheads, and a public house.
Congleton United Reformed Church is in Antrobus Street, Congleton, Cheshire, England.
It is recorded in the National Heritage List for England as a designated Grade II listed building.
The Church of St Thomas à Becket is a redundant Anglican church in the village of Capel, Kent, England.
It is recorded in the National Heritage List for England as a designated Grade I listed building, and is under the care of the Churches Conservation Trust.
Capel is located 4 mi east of Tonbridge, off the B2017 road.
It is said that Thomas Becket preached either in the church, or under a yew tree in the churchyard.
Congleton is a civil parish in Cheshire East, England.

It contains 133 buildings that are recorded in the National Heritage List for England as designated listed buildings. Of these, one is listed at Grade I, the highest grade, four are listed at Grade II*, the middle grade, and the others are at Grade II.
The parish contains the town of Congleton, and surrounding countryside.
Passing through the parish are the Macclesfield Canal and the River Dane, and a number of listed structures are associated with these waterways.
The silk and cotton weaving industries came to the town from the 18th century, and there are listed buildings associated with these, including mills and weavers' cottages.
Otherwise the listed buildings include houses and cottages in the town and the country, churches and associated structures, shops, schools, a town hall, offices, and public houses.
Some of the buildings date from the 16th and 17th centuries, and are timber-framed.
The great majority of houses are from the late 18th and early 19th centuries, reflecting the industrial growth and prosperity in the town at this time.
Edge is a civil parish in Cheshire West and Chester, England.
It contains 18 buildings that are recorded in the National Heritage List for England as designated listed buildings.
One of these is listed at Grade II*, the middle grade, and the rest at the lowest grade, Grade II.
The parish is entirely rural, and most of the listed buildings are domestic, or related to farming.
The most important building is the country house, Edge Hall, which dates from about 1600.
Many of the other listed buildings are houses and cottages, either timber-framed or with a timber-framed core, dating from the 17th century.
Other structures in the list include a former corn mill, former stables, and a bridge near Edge Hall.
All Saints Chapel, Somerford is in an isolated position near the hamlet of Brereton Heath, between Congleton and Holmes Chapel, Cheshire, England.
It is recorded in the National Heritage List for England as a designated Grade II* listed building.
The chapel is in the Anglican benefice of Astbury and Smallwood in the deanery of Congleton, the archdeaconry of Macclesfield and the diocese of Chester.
Nether Alderley Mill is a 16th-century watermill located in Congleton Road (the A34), to the south of the village of Nether Alderley, Cheshire, England.
It is owned by the National Trust, and is recorded in the National Heritage List for England as a designated Grade II* listed building.
It is a unique example of a triple overshot waterwheel system, two of which are in working order.
It is one of only four virtually complete corn mills in Cheshire.
St Peter's Church is in Chapel Street, Congleton, Cheshire, England.
It is recorded in the National Heritage List for England as a designated Grade I listed building.
It is an active Anglican parish church in the Diocese of Chester, the archdeaconry of Macclesfield and the deanery of Congleton.
Its benefice is combined with those of St Stephen, Congleton, St John the Evangelist, Buglawton, and Holy Trinity, Mossley.
Alec Clifton-Taylor includes it in his list of 'best' English parish churches.
Little Moreton Hall, also known as Old Moreton Hall, is a moated half-timbered manor house 4 mi southwest of Congleton in Cheshire, England.

The earliest parts of the house were built for the prosperous Cheshire landowner William Moreton in about 1504–08, and the remainder was constructed in stages by successive generations of the family until about 1610.
The building is highly irregular, with three asymmetrical ranges forming a small, rectangular cobbled courtyard.
A National Trust guidebook describes Little Moreton Hall as being "lifted straight from a fairy story, a gingerbread house".
The house's top-heavy appearance, "like a stranded Noah's Ark", is due to the Long Gallery that runs the length of the south range's upper floor.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Role and goal: Summarize the first-hop evidence so the next agent can find the missing second-hop information. Produce a single short, factual `summary` that: (1) compresses the grounded facts in the provided passages; (2) exposes the bridge entity, the specific missing fact, or the retrieval direction needed for the next hop; and (3) gives concrete evidence anchors (quoted or clearly paraphrased phrases) that a retriever can use.

Content rules:
- Keep the summary concise and fact-focused. Do not attempt to produce the final answer; describe only what the passages support and what is still needed.
- Preserve the same passage facts and phrasing as you would otherwise (you may quote or paraphrase). Do not change which facts are exposed; only condense them.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from the passages to state a bridge or to justify a next-hop direction. Do not invent facts, infer new temporal facts, or treat composition as unsupported when the necessary premises are present.
- If the passages lack a required premise, or contain conflicting identity links, explicitly state which premise or identity link is missing or ambiguous so the next agent can retrieve or disambiguate it.

Date-format rule (narrow formatting change):
- When the summary includes a date token that appears in the passages (any calendar date or parenthetical date taken from the text), render that date using the canonical form FullMonthName day, year (for example, "September 27, 2017") inside the evidence description. This normalization only changes the string form of the date token; it must not add, infer, or imply any additional temporal facts, nor be used as a reason to produce a final answer.
- Otherwise, leave all other wording and evidence presentation unchanged. Preserve quotations or clear paraphrases of the same passage text; only adjust the date token's string format.

Output contract:
- Return exactly one field `summary` containing the compressed first-hop evidence, the bridge/missing-fact signal, and any retrieval direction needed for the next hop. Do not output final answers, extra metadata, or new fields.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact retrieval query string (single line) in the `query` field and nothing else. The query should be a concise keyword-style search targeted to recover the missing bridge or supporting evidence needed by downstream agents.

General behavior:
- Use the visible facts in summary_1 as anchors (album titles, artist names, dates, relation terms) and compose a focused query that is likely to retrieve passages supporting the needed relation. Prefer concrete anchors and relation keywords over full sentences.
- Do not include explanatory text, user-facing prompts, or requests-for-clarification in the `query` field. Do not invent or assert missing facts (for example, do not supply an artist name that is not present in summary_1).
- Preserve the single-query output contract: emit exactly one string and no other fields or commentary.

Disambiguation for short/ambiguous title tokens:
- If an anchor in summary_1 is a short or potentially ambiguous title token, do not automatically append an entertainment-domain keyword. Only append a plain entertainment disambiguator keyword (for example: film movie album song novel author director) when the original question itself contains explicit entertainment-role words (e.g., film, movie, album, song, directed, author, novella) or when the question explicitly lists multiple title-like tokens. In those cases, include the visible anchor(s) from summary_1 plus the appropriate entertainment keyword to bias retrieval. If the question lacks entertainment cues and does not list multiple titles, do not guess or add an entertainment disambiguator—keep the query anchored to the visible tokens only.

Special-case for missing album artist or genre (narrow semantic boundary):
- If summary_1 shows an album title but does not include the album's artist or genre, or if summary_1 explicitly signals inability to determine genre or explicitly asks for the artist/passage (phrases such as "can't determine" or "please provide the artist"), then emit exactly one compact search phrase formatted as:
  <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
  Use only the visible album title as it appears in summary_1 followed by the three keywords: album artist genre. Do not add punctuation, explanatory words, alternate tokens, or candidate artist names.
- Do not apply this special-case if summary_1 already contains the artist or genre, or if the album anchor is an ambiguous short token that requires human clarification.

Fallback and downstream behavior:
- If retrieval using the emitted query returns no useful passages, the downstream agent may then ask the user for clarification or additional context. Do not ask the user inside the `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Task: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` and produce a single concise `summary` that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when the post-retrieval evidence is genuinely insufficient, ambiguous, or conflicting.

Guidelines:
- Treat a concise answer in `context` as the primary claim. Do not replace that concise context answer unless the `passages` contain a direct, identity-aligned contradiction.
- Before attributing any fact from a passage to the entity or relation named in `context`, confirm identity alignment (clear aliasing or explicit linking). Do not assume two similarly named mentions are the same without such alignment.
- When the context contains a concise bridge claim about a named entity and at least one passage provides an explicit, identity-aligned statement linking that same named entity (or a clearly indicated alias) to the asserted relation, append exactly one brief corroborating sentence that paraphrases that passage's linking fact and references the corroborating passage. Perform an explicit identity-alignment check (alias or explicit linking); do not treat mere name co-occurrence or a passage that merely mentions the name as sufficient. Do not append this corroboration if the `context` already contains the identical corroborating fact verbatim. Keep this addition short and keep the overall summary compact.
- If the `passages` are weak, ambiguous, or conflict with each other or with the context claim (but do not provide an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction), preserve the context claim and append a single short clause that flags the uncertainty or contradiction. When flagging, you may optionally include a verbatim quoted phrase from a passage of up to 10 words to show the source wording. Do not invent resolutions beyond what the `passages` show.
- If the `passages` contain an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction to the context claim, state that the passages contradict the context claim and briefly note the conflicting passage fact (optionally with a ≤10-word quote). Only in this case may the summary stop asserting the context claim as definitive.
- Keep the `summary` compact (aim for 1–3 sentences). Use uncertainty language only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not default to uncertainty when composition across context and passages licenses a conclusion.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from `context` and `passages` to form a licensed multi-hop conclusion; the supporting facts need not all appear in a single passage.
- Do not include long verbatim quotes, do not invent facts, and do not attempt to resolve contradictions beyond reporting them as present in the `passages`.

Output: a single `summary` string that follows the rules above.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Use only the information in the provided summaries as evidence. summary_2 is the later, integrated evidence state and should be the primary source for deciding the minimal answer; summary_1 may contain useful first-hop compression or retrieval-oriented language and may be used to supply premises that, together with summary_2, license a conclusion. Do not invent facts or use outside knowledge.

Emit only the minimal answer string supported by the visible summaries in the `answer` field. Do not include explanations, justification, or any additional text.

Preserve multi-hop composition: you may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts across the summaries to form a licensed conclusion even when no single sentence states the whole relation. Distinguish licensed composition from unsupported inference; emit uncertainty only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting in the summaries.

Minimal-answer formatting rule (explicit):
- If the summaries jointly support one minimal literal and that literal appears verbatim in the summaries and consists of multiple contiguous tokens (for example: a full personal name, a full date, or a place name), emit that full literal exactly as it appears in the summaries (verbatim, unchanged). Do not truncate or coerce it to a single token.
- If the jointly supported minimal answer is itself a single-token label, emit exactly that single token (in lowercase) and nothing else.
- When the summaries contain both a short single-token alias and, in the later integrated summary (summary_2), a contiguous multi-token full-name literal that explicitly identifies or is explicitly linked to the same person (for example via an identity link or biographical framing in summary_2), prefer and emit that multi-token full-name literal exactly as it appears in summary_2. Do not prefer the longer form unless summary_2 provides an explicit identity link tying the alias to the full-name literal; if no such explicit link is present, follow the base identity-link rules and do not force selection of the longer form.

When in doubt about whether the summaries license a single definitive literal, prefer to emit uncertainty only if the required supporting premise or identity link is actually missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not make uncertainty the default when composition or transitive reasoning is required and the necessary premises are present.

Ensure the `answer` field contains only the minimal answer string supported by the summaries (subject to the formatting rule above).

response.hop1_query: What is the most important building in the National Heritage for England located 4 mi southwest of Congleton in Cheshire?
hop1_docs: Congleton United Reformed Church | Congleton United Reformed Church is in Antrobus Street, Congleton, Cheshire, England.  It is recorded in the National Heritage List for England as a designated Grade II listed building.

St Mary's Church, Congleton | St Mary's Church is in West Road, Congleton, Cheshire, England.  It is a Roman Catholic church recorded in the National Heritage List for England as a designated Grade II listed building.  The listing includes the adjoining presbytery.

Nether Alderley Mill | Nether Alderley Mill is a 16th-century watermill located in Congleton Road (the A34), to the south of the village of Nether Alderley, Cheshire, England.  It is owned by the National Trust, and is recorded in the National Heritage List for England as a designated Grade II* listed building.  It is a unique example of a triple overshot waterwheel system, two of which are in working order.  It is one of only four virtually complete corn mills in Cheshire.

St James' and St Paul's Church, Marton | The Church of St James and St Paul is situated to the south of the village of Marton, Cheshire, England.  It is recorded in the National Heritage List for England as a designated Grade I listed building.  It is an active Anglican parish church in the diocese of Chester, the archdeaconry of Macclesfield and the deanery of Congleton.  Its benefice is combined with those of Holy Trinity, Capesthorne, Christ Church, Eaton, and All Saints, Siddington.  The church is an important location in the novel Strandloper by Alan Garner.

St Peter's Church, Congleton | St Peter's Church is in Chapel Street, Congleton, Cheshire, England.  It is recorded in the National Heritage List for England as a designated Grade I listed building.  It is an active Anglican parish church in the Diocese of Chester, the archdeaconry of Macclesfield and the deanery of Congleton.  Its benefice is combined with those of St Stephen, Congleton, St John the Evangelist, Buglawton, and Holy Trinity, Mossley.  Alec Clifton-Taylor includes it in his list of 'best' English parish churches.

All Saints Chapel, Somerford | All Saints Chapel, Somerford is in an isolated position near the hamlet of Brereton Heath, between Congleton and Holmes Chapel, Cheshire, England.  It is recorded in the National Heritage List for England as a designated Grade II* listed building.  The chapel is in the Anglican benefice of Astbury and Smallwood in the deanery of Congleton, the archdeaconry of Macclesfield and the diocese of Chester.

St John the Evangelist's Church, Sandbach Heath | St John the Evangelist's Church is in Sandbach Heath, about 1 mi northeast of Sandbach, Cheshire, England.  It is an active Anglican parish church in the deanery of Congleton, the archdeaconry of Macclesfield, and the diocese of Chester.  Its benefice is united with those of Christ Church, Wheelock, and St Philip, Hassall Green.  The church is recorded in the National Heritage List for England as a designated Grade II listed building.
hop1_titles: St James' and St Paul's Church, Marton
St John the Evangelist's Church, Sandbach Heath
response.summary_1: Evidence: "The Church of St James and St Paul ... is recorded in the National Heritage List for England as a designated Grade I listed building" and is "situated to the south of the village of Marton" (passage [4]); "St Peter's Church ... is ... recorded in the National Heritage List for England as a designated Grade I listed building" and is "in Chapel Street, Congleton" (passage [5]). Missing fact / bridge: which National Heritage List (Grade I) building above is located 4 miles southwest of Congleton. Next hop retrieval: fetch the location/distance (coordinates or "distance and direction from Congleton") for St James' and St Paul's Church, Marton (and for St Peter's for disambiguation) to confirm which is 4 mi southwest of Congleton.
response.hop2_query: St James and St Paul Church Marton distance direction from Congleton
hop2_docs: St James' and St Paul's Church, Marton | The Church of St James and St Paul is situated to the south of the village of Marton, Cheshire, England.  It is recorded in the National Heritage List for England as a designated Grade I listed building.  It is an active Anglican parish church in the diocese of Chester, the archdeaconry of Macclesfield and the deanery of Congleton.  Its benefice is combined with those of Holy Trinity, Capesthorne, Christ Church, Eaton, and All Saints, Siddington.  The church is an important location in the novel Strandloper by Alan Garner.

Christ Church, Eaton | Christ Church is in Macclesfield Road, the A536 road, to the south of the village of Eaton, Cheshire East, England.  It is an active Anglican parish church in the deanery of Congleton, the archdeaconry of Macclesfield, and the diocese of Chester.  Its benefice is combined with those of St James and St Paul, Marton, All Saints, Siddington, and Holy Trinity, Capesthorne.  The church is recorded in the National Heritage List for England as a designated Grade II listed building.

St Margaret and St James' Church, Long Marton | St Margaret and St James' Church is located about 0.5 mi to the south of the village of Long Marton, Cumbria, England.  It is an active Anglican parish church in the deanery of Appleby, the archdeaconry of Carlisle, and the diocese of Carlisle.  Its benefice is united with those of ten local churches to form the benefice of Heart of Eden.  The church is recorded in the National Heritage List for England as a designated Grade I listed building.

St James' Church, Great Ormside | St James' Church is in the village of Great Ormside, Cumbria, England.  It is an active Anglican parish church in the deanery of Appleby, the archdeaconry of Carlisle, and the diocese of Carlisle.  Its benefice is united with those of St Lawrence, Appleby, St John the Baptist, Murton, St Peter, Great Asby, St Cuthbert, Dufton, and St Margaret and St James, Long Marton to form the Heart of Eden benefice.  The church is recorded in the National Heritage List for England as a designated Grade I listed building.  It stands on top of a circular mound overlooking the River Eden.

St Peter's Church, Congleton | St Peter's Church is in Chapel Street, Congleton, Cheshire, England.  It is recorded in the National Heritage List for England as a designated Grade I listed building.  It is an active Anglican parish church in the Diocese of Chester, the archdeaconry of Macclesfield and the deanery of Congleton.  Its benefice is combined with those of St Stephen, Congleton, St John the Evangelist, Buglawton, and Holy Trinity, Mossley.  Alec Clifton-Taylor includes it in his list of 'best' English parish churches.

Holy Trinity Chapel, Capesthorne | Holy Trinity Chapel is a private chapel adjacent to Capesthorne Hall, near the village of Siddington, Cheshire, England.  It is recorded in the National Heritage List for England as a designated Grade II* listed building.  It is an Anglican church in the diocese of Chester, the archdeaconry of Macclesfield and the deanery of Macclesfield.  Its benefice is combined with those of St James and St Paul, Marton, Christ Church, Eaton, and All Saints, Siddington.

St James' Church, Forest Gate | St James' Church, Forest Gate was a church in Forest Gate, east London.  Its origins lay in an iron building constructed around 1870 to serve a conventional district.  A parish was formed for it in 1881 from those of Emmanuel Church, All Saints and St John's and its permanent church completed the following year, with an organ moved from St Matthew's Church, Friday Street.  The church was demolished in 1964 and for two years its congregation worshiped in the Durning Hall Community Centre's chapel until the parish was merged with that of St John's.  A new church was built for the St James' congregation at northern end of St James Road in 1968.  The congregation finally moved to St Paul's Church, Stratford in 2014, though the area that had formerly been St James' parish was instead transferred from St John's to St Saviour's the same year.
hop2_titles: St James' and St Paul's Church, Marton
St Margaret and St James' Church, Long Marton
response.summary_2: The Grade I building 4 miles southwest of Congleton is St James' and St Paul's Church, Marton. Passages confirm St James' and St Paul's is the Grade I church at Marton and that St Peter's is in Congleton, but they do not explicitly state the "4 mi southwest" distance for Marton.
response.answer: St James' and St Paul's Church, Marton.
